summary: DeVault criticizes PINE64 for betraying its community-driven development model, triggered by postmarketOS developer Martijn Braam's departure and PINE64's dismissive response. He argues that PINE64's shift toward a Manjaro mono-culture undermines the diverse ecosystem that made their hardware viable. The post outlines specific demands including apologies, reinstating community editions, and rescinding the Manjaro-exclusive commitment. DeVault frames three possible outcomes: community decay and PINE64's demise, grudging change without accountability, or graceful acknowledgment and reform. As a long-time supporter and owner of multiple PINE64 devices, he makes clear his continued support is conditional on meaningful change.
key_points: PINE64's dismissive response to Martijn Braam's departure post demonstrates a failure to take responsibility for community mistreatment
The shift to a Manjaro mono-culture threatens the diverse community ecosystem that PINE64 depends on to make its hardware functional
Manjaro has a documented history of financial mismanagement, security incidents, and broken software that makes it unfit for a privileged position
Even PINE64's most mature products still need significant community work, and newer products are not remotely usable without community support
DeVault presents three possible futures: community collapse, grudging reform without accountability, or graceful humility and genuine recommitment
money_quotes: I know that apologising and taking responsibility for your mistakes is difficult. It seems especially difficult for commercial endeavours, which have fostered a culture of cold disassociation from responsibility for their actions.
The future of PINE64's Manjaro mono-culture is dim.

Manjaro is a very poorly run Linux distribution with a history of financial mismanagement, ethical violations, security incidents, shipping broken software, and disregarding the input of its peers in the distribution community.
Once they turn their back on the lush ecosystem they promised, I no longer have any interest in the platform.
If PINE64 refuses to change course, the community will continue to decay and fail — the community PINE64 depends on to make its devices functional and useful.
This course of events will be the end of PINE64 and deal a terrible blow to the mobile FOSS movement.
Remember, the community are volunteers. They have no obligation to make peace, so it's on you to mend these wounds.
key_insight: A hardware company that depends on volunteer community contributions to make its products functional cannot afford to alienate that community by consolidating around a single poorly-run distribution and dismissing legitimate grievances.
